name: Climate Stress Test Frameworks for Financial Institutions
description: Regulatory climate stress test frameworks from ECB, Bank of England, and NGFS, including scenario parameters, required outputs, and reporting formats.

scope.institutions: All significant institutions (SIs) under ECB direct supervision (~104 banks)
scope.portfolioCoverage: All material exposures including corporate lending, mortgages, sovereign, and market portfolios
scope.granularity: Counterparty-level for top exposures, sector-level for remainder

scenarios.name: Orderly Transition
scenarios.description: Early, ambitious policy action. Carbon prices rise steadily. Physical risks limited.

scenarios.name: Disorderly Transition
scenarios.description: Delayed action followed by sudden, severe policy tightening after 2030. Sharp carbon price spike.

scenarios.name: Hot House World
scenarios.description: No additional policy action. Severe physical risks materialize. GDP severely impacted long-term.

requiredOutputs.description: Expected credit losses (ECL) under each scenario by sector and geography
Mark-to-market losses on securities and derivatives portfolios
Qualitative assessment of physical risk exposure to own operations
Impact on net interest income and fee income from transition

reportingFormat.reviewProcess: ECB supervisory assessment, results published in aggregate

frameworks.name: Bank of England Climate Biennial Exploratory Scenario (CBES)
frameworks.issuingBody: Bank of England / Prudential Regulation Authority

scope.institutions: Major UK banks and insurers (~19 firms in 2021: 7 banks/building societies, 12 insurers)
scope.portfolioCoverage: UK and global exposures. Banks: lending portfolios. Insurers: investment and underwriting portfolios.

scenarios.name: Early Action (EA)
scenarios.description: Strong, early policy action. Gradual transition. Physical risks limited. Manageable financial impact.

scenarios.name: Late Action (LA)
scenarios.description: Delayed policy action until 2031. Sudden, sharp tightening required. Higher transition costs and financial disruption.

scenarios.name: No Additional Action (NAA)
scenarios.description: No new climate policy. Severe physical risks by 2050. Chronic GDP loss, particularly in vulnerable regions.

requiredOutputs.description: Projected credit impairments across NACE sectors under each scenario
Impact on traded asset valuations including equities, bonds, and derivatives
Changes in claims frequency/severity for property, casualty, life insurance
Strategic assessment of business model resilience and adaptation plans

reportingFormat.templates: BoE standardized templates with qualitative questionnaires
reportingFormat.reviewProcess: PRA supervisory review, aggregate results published. Not used for capital requirements.

frameworks.name: NGFS Climate Scenarios (Phase IV)
frameworks.issuingBody: Network for Greening the Financial System

scope.purpose: Reference scenarios for central banks and financial supervisors to assess climate-related financial risks
scope.variables: Macro-financial variables (GDP, investment, energy prices), physical risk variables, transition risk variables

scenarios.name: Net Zero 2050
scenarios.description: Stringent climate policies and innovation reaching net zero CO2 emissions around 2050. Gives 50% chance of limiting warming to 1.5°C.

scenarios.name: Below 2°C
scenarios.description: Gradual strengthening of climate policies giving a 67% chance of limiting warming to below 2°C.

scenarios.name: Divergent Net Zero
scenarios.description: Reaches net zero by 2050 but with higher costs due to divergent policies across sectors. More disruptive, with limited CCS and greater reliance on demand reduction.

scenarios.name: Delayed Transition
scenarios.description: No new climate policies until 2030. Strong policies then compensate, requiring sharply higher carbon prices. Significant financial disruption.

scenarios.name: Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs)
scenarios.description: Countries implement current NDC pledges but no further ambition. Physical risks become significant by 2050.

scenarios.name: Current Policies
scenarios.description: Only currently implemented policies. No further action. Leads to 3°C+ warming by 2100. Severe physical risks. Large GDP losses.

requiredOutputs.description: GDP, investment, consumption, government revenue projections by country/region
Energy prices, generation mix, final energy demand by fuel and sector
Temperature, precipitation, sea level, extreme event frequency by region
Carbon prices, policy stringency, technology costs, stranded assets

reportingFormat.documentation: NGFS Technical Documentation, published with each phase
reportingFormat.updateCycle: Annual updates with new model runs
